title: CDMGRestrictions
lean_code_with_comments:
theorem no_hus_into_J (G : CDMG Node) {j : Node} (hj : j ∈ G.J) (v : Node) :
    ¬ G.hus j v
-- claim_3_1 -- end statement
  := by
    intro h
    rcases h with h_hut | h_huh
    · exact Finset.disjoint_left.mp G.hJV_disj hj (G.hE_subset h_hut).2
    · exact Finset.disjoint_left.mp G.hJV_disj hj (G.hL_subset h_huh).1

theorem tuh_from_J_target_in_V (G : CDMG Node) {j : Node} (_hj : j ∈ G.J)
    {v : Node} (h : G.tuh j v) : v ∈ G.V
-- claim_3_1 -- end statement
  := (G.hE_subset h).2

theorem no_J_J_adjacent (G : CDMG Node) {j1 j2 : Node}
    (hj1 : j1 ∈ G.J) (hj2 : j2 ∈ G.J) (_hne : j1 ≠ j2) :
    ¬ G.adjacent j1 j2
-- claim_3_1 -- end statement
  := by
    intro h
    rcases h with h_tuh | h_hut | h_huh
    · exact Finset.disjoint_left.mp G.hJV_disj hj2 (G.hE_subset h_tuh).2
    · exact Finset.disjoint_left.mp G.hJV_disj hj1 (G.hE_subset h_hut).2
    · exact Finset.disjoint_left.mp G.hJV_disj hj1 (G.hL_subset h_huh).1
lean_code_without_comments:
theorem no_hus_into_J (G : CDMG Node) {j : Node} (hj : j ∈ G.J) (v : Node) :
    ¬ G.hus j v

  := by
    intro h
    rcases h with h_hut | h_huh
    · exact Finset.disjoint_left.mp G.hJV_disj hj (G.hE_subset h_hut).2
    · exact Finset.disjoint_left.mp G.hJV_disj hj (G.hL_subset h_huh).1

theorem tuh_from_J_target_in_V (G : CDMG Node) {j : Node} (_hj : j ∈ G.J)
    {v : Node} (h : G.tuh j v) : v ∈ G.V

  := (G.hE_subset h).2

theorem no_J_J_adjacent (G : CDMG Node) {j1 j2 : Node}
    (hj1 : j1 ∈ G.J) (hj2 : j2 ∈ G.J) (_hne : j1 ≠ j2) :
    ¬ G.adjacent j1 j2

  := by
    intro h
    rcases h with h_tuh | h_hut | h_huh
    · exact Finset.disjoint_left.mp G.hJV_disj hj2 (G.hE_subset h_tuh).2
    · exact Finset.disjoint_left.mp G.hJV_disj hj1 (G.hE_subset h_hut).2
    · exact Finset.disjoint_left.mp G.hJV_disj hj1 (G.hL_subset h_huh).1
lean_explanation:
The remark formalises three structural restrictions read off the `CDMG` definition. Each Lean theorem corresponds to one LN sentence and lives inside the `CDMG` namespace, parameterised by an ambient vertex type `Node` with decidable equality. The receiver `G : CDMG Node` exposes the structural projections used in every proof: `hJV_disj : Disjoint G.J G.V`, `hE_subset` (the directed-edge constraint $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$), and `hL_subset` (the bidirected-edge constraint $L \subseteq V \times V$).

In `no_hus_into_J` the hypothesis `hj : j ∈ G.J` pins `j` to the intervention layer, and the explicit binder `(v : Node)` makes visible the universal quantifier that the LN leaves implicit in $j \hus v \notin G$. The Mathlib idiom `Finset.disjoint_left.mp G.hJV_disj hj` converts the disjointness witness into the negative membership `j ∉ G.V`. The proof unfolds `hus` to its two-disjunct shape `hut ∨ huh` (from `def_3_2`) via `rcases`, and each disjunct's projection (`hE_subset.2` or `hL_subset.1`) supplies the contradicting `j ∈ G.V`.

`tuh_from_J_target_in_V` is a one-line term-mode proof: `(G.hE_subset h).2` extracts the target-side conjunct of the directed-edge membership. The implicit `{v : Node}` binder mirrors the implicit quantifier in the LN, and the hypothesis `_hj` is retained (leading underscore silences the unused-variable warning) so the signature reads like the LN's *Nodes $j \in J$ can only point towards nodes $v \in V$*.

`no_J_J_adjacent` accepts two `J`-members and an inequality `_hne : j1 ≠ j2`, concluding `¬ G.adjacent j1 j2`. The `adjacent` predicate (from `def_3_3`) unfolds to a three-way `sus` disjunction; each `rcases` branch closes by `hE_subset` / `hL_subset` projection plus `hJV_disj`, repeating the same membership-conflict pattern as part one. None of the three proofs invokes a Mathlib graph lemma — the CDMG type has no first-class Mathlib analogue, so the work is short case analysis on bespoke structure fields.
design_choices:
**Each LN sentence becomes its own theorem.** The remark is a single English paragraph containing three structurally distinct claims: no incoming arrowheads at $J$-nodes, outgoing arrowhead targets must lie in $V$, and pairwise non-adjacency inside $J$. Bundling them into one conjunctive proposition would obscure which sub-fact downstream consumers actually `apply`, so the formalisation splits them into `no_hus_into_J`, `tuh_from_J_target_in_V`, and `no_J_J_adjacent`, named in LN sentence order.

**Only the restrictive half of sentence 2 is formalised.** The LN says *edges $j \tuh v$ are allowed*, which reads like a positive permission; an explicit operator addition to the LN clarifies that this is permissive — *its presence is not asserted, only that no restriction forbids it.* A positive existence claim such as `∃ v, G.tuh j v` would overshoot the LN content and burden every downstream consumer with a redundant witness obligation. The Lean theorem therefore encodes only the restrictive direction `G.tuh j v → v ∈ G.V`; the permissive direction is already folded into the type by the absence of any further constraint on `E` beyond `hE_subset`.

**The distinctness binder `_hne : j1 ≠ j2` is kept even though `¬ G.adjacent j j` is also provable.** In mathematical English *no two nodes* carries implicit distinctness, so the LN claim space is $\{(j_1, j_2) : j_1 \neq j_2\}$, not all of $J \times J$. The self-loop case is a separate structural fact — a corollary of sentence 1 plus the edge subset restrictions — and folding it into this theorem would conflate two distinct LN sentences. A future consumer needing the self-loop variant can introduce a separately named companion lemma at the call site.

**`G.adjacent` is preferred to inlining the three-disjunct `sus` expression.** Spelling the conclusion as `¬ G.adjacent j1 j2` keeps the LN vocabulary (*two nodes adjacent*) visible at the Lean level; inlining the `sus = tuh ∨ hut ∨ huh` expansion would force every reader to unfold the definition before matching the LN sentence to its Lean encoding.

**Implicit versus explicit `v` binders.** `no_hus_into_J` uses an explicit `(v : Node)` to mirror the LN's universally-quantified phrasing $j \hus v \notin G$. `tuh_from_J_target_in_V` uses an implicit `{v : Node}` because the hypothesis `G.tuh j v` already pins `v` at the use site, sparing every caller a trivial explicit instantiation.
